video_title: Saylor's Lesson, Zcash's Crash & AI Coming for Crypto - The Chopping Block
gist: The Chopping Block panel (Tom, Ilia of Near, Mert of Helius, host from Dragonfly) dissects a weekend market meltdown triggered by Michael Saylor's tiny 32-BTC sale plus a hot jobs report, the ~50%+ intraday Zcash crash from an AI-assisted ZK soundness bug, and the structural thesis that AI now permanently tilts crypto's attack/defense asymmetry toward attackers, making formal verification and real-time AI anomaly detection table stakes. They close on Near's bet that AI agents transacting trustlessly on-chain (intents, private inference) is the next platform.

themes.theme: Saylor, MicroStrategy and the DAT Reflexivity Risk

insights.claim: Michael Saylor's sale of just 32 BTC (~$3M-plus) was enough to send the market into a tailspin, signaling that MicroStrategy's 'buy-only' credibility is the load-bearing assumption holding up the trade and that even a tiny test sale breaks the narrative.
insights.speaker: host (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: The market polices Saylor's no-sell promise; any signal he might sell is a sentiment trigger far larger than the dollar amount implies.
insights.quote: There's no sell button for you. You're buy you're buy only

insights.claim: MicroStrategy's Stretch preferred shares are designed to trade around a $100 upward peg via rising dividends but fell to ~94-95 and trade near 97-98, signaling market doubt either that Saylor will pay the dividends or that MicroStrategy could go bust.
insights.speaker: host (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Stretch trading below its $100 peg is a real-time credit-stress gauge for MicroStrategy; watch the discount as a solvency/dividend-credibility signal.
insights.quote: It's designed to trade around $100... This thing is trading below $100. It went all the way down to like 95 94

insights.claim: Mert argues Saylor is a 'red herring' whose real damage is a distortion field that reduces all of crypto's discourse to one man's buys and sells, crowding out adoption, philosophy and tech narratives at the exact moment AI is advancing.
insights.speaker: Mert
insights.so_what: Frames Saylor-centric attention as a narrative liability that deters new entrants; a leadership/storytelling vacuum is a soft headwind for Bitcoin demand.
insights.quote: a trillion people pocket watching one guy which I think is not a very exciting thing

insights.claim: The panel argues spot Bitcoin ETFs and MicroStrategy are structurally the same retail-driven flow mechanism (retail buys IBIT/MSTR shares, APs move the BTC), so 'BlackRock bought' or 'Saylor bought' both really mean retail, not new institutional money.
insights.speaker: host (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Reframes 2024-25 demand as retail reflexivity rather than durable institutional adoption, implying fragility if retail flows reverse.

insights.claim: Ilia argues last year's Bitcoin bull market was largely MicroStrategy buying, reflecting crypto's pattern where 'one thing that worked will always work until it completely fails,' so the market over-relied on MSTR as the sole driver.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: A reflexivity warning: concentration on a single demand driver sets up sharp reversals when that driver stalls.
insights.quote: one thing that worked will always work until until it completely fails and everybody freaks out

insights.claim: Mert contends DATs (digital-asset treasury vehicles) still make sense but only if entered at the bottom not the top, citing the Cipherpunk Zcash DAT as still profitable versus those that bought Ethereum on Tom Lee's $180,000 call.
insights.speaker: Mert
insights.so_what: Entry timing, not the DAT structure itself, determines outcomes; the asset and price at inception are the differentiators.

insights.claim: A hot jobs report drove a large NASDAQ tech drawdown on Friday and shifted Fed expectations toward a possible rate hike, with prediction markets pricing roughly 50/50 odds the Fed raises rates this year and cuts now seen as very unlikely.
insights.speaker: host (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: A hawkish Fed repricing is a direct headwind for risk assets including crypto; the 50/50 hike probability is a key watch level.
insights.quote: poly market's pricing roughly 50/50 that the Fed ends up raising rates this year

insights.claim: Bitcoin broke below the psychologically important 60K guardrail (touching ~59K) amid mass liquidations over the weekend before rebounding above 62K, with the host noting AI now seems like the only thing that can save risk markets.
insights.speaker: host (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: The 60K level acts as a sentiment line; the violent down-and-back move signals fragile, liquidation-prone positioning.

themes.theme: Zcash AI-Assisted ZK Bug and Crash

insights.claim: Zcash crashed more than 50% peak-to-trough in a single day (from ~$600 to ~$250) after disclosure of a soundness bug in the Orchard ZK circuit that could have let an attacker mint counterfeit Zcash inside the shielded pool (~30% of supply) and drain it bank-run style.
insights.speaker: host (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: One of the largest single-token drawdowns in a long time; quantifies how a privacy-protocol soundness bug translates directly into existential price risk.
insights.quote: Ze came down more more than 50% peaked to trough over the course of a single day

insights.claim: The bug was found by security researcher Taylor Hornby of Shielded Labs using Anthropic's Opus 4.8 with a custom harness, then patched via an emergency soft fork; Zooko's later disclosure admitted there is no proof the bug was not exploited, only no evidence it was.
insights.speaker: host (Dragonfly)
insights.so_what: Lingering inability to prove non-exploitation keeps tail risk priced in until the forced migration confirms no counterfeiting occurred.
insights.quote: technically we have no proof that this bug was not exploited

insights.claim: Mert argues the bug was not 'vibe-coded by Claude'; Taylor Hornby is a top-five Zcash expert and the circuit had passed prior audits by top cryptographers, so the AI was a guided tool wielded by deep human expertise, not autonomous discovery.
insights.speaker: Mert
insights.so_what: Tempers the 'AI alone found it' narrative; current AI augments elite humans rather than replacing them in finding deep ZK bugs.
insights.quote: one hacker to have found it just by vibe coding would be in my view extremely unlikely

insights.claim: Zcash will migrate from the Orchard pool to a new formally-verified, quantum-recoverable shielded pool called Ironwood in early-to-mid July, using a turnstile migration to prove no counterfeit coins exist; formal verification by three separate teams should finish before go-live.
insights.speaker: Mert
insights.so_what: The July migration is the catalyst that resolves the counterfeiting uncertainty; formal verification across three teams is the bull case for the same bug class being eliminated.

insights.claim: An attempt by Ilia's friend to re-discover the Zcash bug using Opus 4.8 (even hinting 'there's a soundness bug') failed after 30 minutes, illustrating that today's models can confirm a bug when told where it is but cannot reliably search the whole tree for it the way a Mythos-style model might.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Marks the current capability frontier: directed verification works, open-ended deep search does not yet, but more powerful models would close that gap.

themes.theme: AI's Attack/Defense Asymmetry in Crypto Security

insights.claim: Ilia argues AI permanently tilts the attack/defense asymmetry toward attackers because defenders use one model while thousands of attackers run multiple models, ensembles and unlimited time, so the only durable solution is formal verification.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Implies protocols that lack formal verification are structurally exposed; verification becomes a security moat investors should screen for.
insights.quote: the asymmetry that happens between the attack and defense is just always going to be on attacker side

insights.claim: Ilia claims the wave of DeFi hacks starting October/November coincided with new Opus model releases, and a further surge around April came from attackers running open-source models (e.g. DeepSeek) on their own GPUs to hide hacking activity from inference providers.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Ties hack frequency directly to AI capability releases; open-weight models on private GPUs remove a key detection chokepoint, raising baseline protocol risk.
insights.quote: why defi started getting hacked in November October well the oppus came out

insights.claim: Ilia characterizes Anthropic's Mythos as more of a marketing ploy, arguing any security expert with current models can already find vulnerabilities regardless of which model is used, by building a validation loop that runs actual mint/exploit attempts and can even apply reinforcement learning.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Suggests the exploit-discovery capability is already commoditized via tooling/loops, not gated by a single frontier model.

insights.claim: Tom frames a paradigm shift from 'boat' software (expected to take on some water, with pumps) to 'spaceship' software that must be completely airtight, because crypto smart contracts get exploited the moment a vulnerability exists.
insights.speaker: Tom
insights.so_what: Reusable mental model: in adversarial on-chain environments 'roughly secure' equals hacked, raising the bar for what counts as production-grade code.
insights.quote: we're kind of moving to a more spaceship type approach to code where it has to be completely airtight

insights.claim: AI-driven formal verification collapses the historical cost barrier (hundreds of dollars per line, manual, error-prone specification) by letting models generate and validate proofs in a loop, making properties like 'total supply does not change' provable as code is developed.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: If AI makes formal verification cheap and continuous, it could become standard for crypto and high-assurance software, reshaping security spend.

insights.claim: Ilia argues operational security failures (oracles, multisigs, admin keys) regressed as a new wave of builders launched memecoins and contracts with admin keys in text files, only relearning the 2017-18 multisig-hack lessons recently.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: OpSec, not just code correctness, remains a recurring failure mode; due diligence should weight key-management and oracle design heavily.

insights.claim: Ilia argues crypto must move beyond absolute 'code is law' toward real-time anomaly detection, citing Near's system that runs AI analytics on intents (request-response, not atomic) to flag suspicious deposits like funds arriving from a previously empty account.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Positions on-chain ML fraud detection (analogous to JPMorgan card fraud) as a product differentiator and adoption enabler for mainstream users.
insights.quote: we kind of need to get out of this like code is law kind of absolute

insights.claim: Mert resolves the cypherpunk tension by arguing that if you have the means to stop crime you legally and morally must, so the true cypherpunk move is to architect immutable, decentralized systems where you never hold that control in the first place.
insights.speaker: Mert
insights.so_what: A governance test for protocols: claimed decentralization is hollow if operators retain (and decline to use) intervention power; structure determines liability.
insights.quote: if you have the means to stop it and you don't stop it because you're saying you're a cipher punk. Well, first of all, you're not a cypher punk

insights.claim: Ilia notes Near built cross-chain hack detection because stolen funds are laundered through Near Intents, running predictive OpSec on other chains and coordinating war-room shutdowns (e.g. signals from Mert) during the April wave of hacks.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Cross-chain laundering forces infrastructure providers into active defense roles; real-time on-chain detection is becoming a competitive necessity.

insights.claim: Mert is long math, arguing that since computing has continuously improved in security and cryptography and no law of physics forbids a mathematically secure system, many security battles will be lost but the war will ultimately be won.
insights.speaker: Mert
insights.so_what: A long-horizon bull case for crypto surviving the AI-exploit era through error correction and hardening, not a near-term safety claim.
insights.quote: there will be many battles that are lost but fundamentally the war will be won

insights.claim: Ilia frames AI as the single-player interface and blockchain as the multiplayer coordination/settlement layer, with Near positioning intents as a higher abstraction (outcome specification) above raw transactions to power agent-to-agent commerce.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Defines Near's strategic bet; intents-as-abstraction is the product wedge for AI agents settling trustless transactions on-chain.
insights.quote: AI is... the single player... And the blockchain is a multiplayer

insights.claim: Near processed $220-240 million in one-day volume during Friday's market bloodbath, which at current take rate exceeded near token inflation, making it a deflationary day and the core of a buyback-driven token economic model.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Concrete fee-revenue-exceeds-inflation data point supporting Near's deflationary token thesis; volume growth from trading plus commerce is the value driver to track.
insights.quote: we've done 220 40 million in volume which actually based on... was more than inflation of near. So we that was a deflationary day

insights.claim: Ilia argues the real value of on-chain agents is not payments (an agent can just use a credit card) but trustless interaction, escrow, reputation and dispute resolution that open new markets like direct purchasing from Chinese factories.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Reframes agentic commerce away from payments toward trust infrastructure; the moat is escrow/reputation/dispute layers, not stablecoin transfer.
insights.quote: the problem is when you actually need trustless interaction between different parties

insights.claim: Near's private inference stack (used by Venice AI) and Ironclaw run on the same multi-party-computation chain-signatures engine that powers cross-chain swaps, so end-to-end encrypted AI and multi-chain swaps share core tech and can be paid for by credit card.
insights.speaker: Ilia
insights.so_what: Shared MPC infrastructure across AI and swaps is a capital-efficiency and product-surface advantage; Circle partnership extends private agent balances.

insights.claim: Mert publicly tells Ilia to stop talking about AI agents on-chain and focus on trading, saying he does not buy the 'all agents coming on chain' story while still rating Near among the most underrated, slept-on teams in crypto alongside Zcash and Solana.
insights.speaker: Mert
insights.so_what: A credible skeptic endorses the Near team while doubting the agentic-commerce narrative, separating team quality from the specific AI thesis.
insights.quote: I think near is the most underrated team in crypto and I think they are extremely slept on
